1: me polloi didaskaloi ginesthe adelphoi mou eidotes hoti meizon krima lepsometha (mē polloi didaskaloi ginesthe, adelphoi mou, eidotes hoti meizon krima lēpsometha): 'Not many of you should become teachers, my brothers, knowing that we who teach will be judged with greater strictness.' The meizon krima (greater judgment) for teachers reflects the Jewish wisdom tradition's high estimation of the Torah-teacher's responsibility (m. Avot James 2:7: 'the more Torah, the more life; the more study, the more wisdom; the more counsel, the more understanding; the more righteousness, the more peace; ... the more Torah, the more life') and its warning about the consequences of teaching falsehood. James is aware of his own exposure to this judgment.
2: teleios anēr — the one who does not stumble in speech is a teleios anēr (perfect/complete/mature man). The connection to the teleios of 1:4 is deliberate: the mature person of James 1 was to be produced by endurance; here, control of the tongue is the marker of that maturity. The able-to-bridle-the-whole-body metaphor connects speech control to bodily self-governance: mastering the most unruly member (tongue) demonstrates the capacity to master the rest. Kai holon to sōma chalinagōgēsai — the ability to 'bridle the whole body' (chalinagōgēsai, hapax in the NT; from chalinos, bridle + agō, to lead).
3: chalinous eis ta stomata tōn hippōn — bits/bridles into the mouths of horses. The argument moves from the small bit that controls the large horse, to the small rudder that steers the large ship (v4), to the small tongue that sets the course of the whole person's life. The rhetorical technique is synkrisis (comparison of unlike things by their ratio): small controlling large is the pattern that makes the tongue's influence comprehensible. The term metago (we direct their whole bodies) uses a compound that emphasizes transfer: the bit transfers the rider's will to the horse's entire movement.
4: euthynōn hormē — the ship is steered by a very small rudder wherever the impulse of the one steering wills (hopou hē hormē tou euthynontos bouletai). Hormē (impulse/inclination) is the navigator's directing intention translated into the rudder's movement. The nautical term euthynōn (one who steers straight) may echo the same nautical vocabulary in Greek wisdom literature about self-governance (Stoic metaphors of the steering reason). James's argument is that the rudder of speech similarly encodes the soul's deepest inclinations and translates them into life's direction.
5: glōssa mikron melos estin kai megala auchei — the tongue is a small member and yet boasts great things (megala auchei). The verb auchei (boasts/brags) — from aucheo — depicts speech not merely as communication but as self-assertion. The forest-fire image (hylikon pyr anaptei, sets a great forest on fire) is vivid: James has just described the tongue's disproportionate influence (small bit, small rudder, small tongue) and now captures the destructive version of that disproportionality. The small match ignites a wildfire that cannot be recalled.
6: kosmos tēs adikias / ton trochon tēs geneseos — the tongue is a 'world of unrighteousness' (kosmos tēs adikias) among our members and 'sets on fire the wheel of existence' (ton trochon tēs geneseos phlogizousa). The second phrase is the most debated in James: trochon tēs geneseos (wheel of genesis/becoming/birth) may echo the Orphic doctrine of the wheel of reincarnation, or more plausibly the cycle of human life from birth to death. The tongue defiles (spilousa, stains) the whole body, sets the whole course of life ablaze, and is itself set on fire by Gehenna (geenna, the only NT use of geenna outside the Gospels in this sense).
7: physis tōn thēriōn te kai peteinōn — every kind of animal — beasts, birds, reptiles, sea creatures — has been tamed (damazetai, tamed/subdued) and is being tamed by human physis (nature). The fourfold list echoes the four categories of Genesis 1:26-28 over which God gave humanity dominion (creatures of the land, air, ground-creeping, sea). James's point is ironic: humanity exercises the Gen 1 dominion mandate over the entire animal creation but cannot tame its own tongue.
8: akatastaton kakon gemei iou thanatēphorou — an unruly/restless evil (akatastaton kakon — akatastatos appears in 1:8 for the double-minded person; the tongue has the same instability), full (gemei) of deadly poison (iou thanatēphorou — ios means both poison/venom and rust; here the death-bearing poison of a serpent or arrow). The tongue's self-description as 'full of deadly poison' echoes the Psalm tradition: Psalms 140:3 ('they make their tongues as sharp as a serpent's; the poison of vipers is on their lips') and Psalms 58:4 ('their venom is like the venom of a snake').
9: kath' homoiōsin theou — made in the likeness (homoiōsis) of God. The term picks up Genesis 1:26-27 (both eikōn and homoiōsis are used in the LXX of Genesis 1:26). To curse a person made in God's likeness is to curse the image of God — it is an indirect blasphemy against God himself. This makes the double-use of the tongue (blessing God, cursing image-bearers) internally incoherent: to use the same mouth for both is to address the same divine reality with opposite attitudes, which is exactly the dipsychos (double-mindedness) James condemns throughout the letter.
10: ou chrē... ginesthai — this should not be (ou chrē, it is not fitting/proper/necessary — a mild but firm expression of inappropriateness). The grammar is impersonal (chrē, 'it is fitting') rather than direct imperative, making the observation almost logical: these things ought not to coexist in nature. The blending of blessing and cursing from one source is as unnatural as a spring producing both fresh and salt water (v11) or a tree producing incompatible fruits (v12). The moral impossibility is argued by analogy to natural impossibility.
11: glykos kai pikron — sweet water and bitter (glykys kai pikros) from the same spring. The paradox of natural springs is used as an analogy for the tongue's incompatible productions. In the ancient world, the freshwater/saltwater boundary was a real concern for wells near the sea coast; a spring that produced both would be useless. Brein (to gush/bubble forth) is a vivid verb for the natural overflow of a spring — the tongue should naturally overflow with consistent water.
12: elasthē hypo sklērou anemou — although driven by fierce winds (sklērou anemou, hard/harsh wind). The same contrast of size and power as v3-4: the ship is large, the winds are fierce, the rudder is small. James's consistency in using nautical and natural imagery makes the tongue-section a unified argument from proportion and nature to the disproportionate power and inconsistency of speech. The fig tree and grapevine image (oute ampelon syka poiēsai) draws on the OT's frequent use of vineyards and fig trees as images for Israel's prosperity and faithfulness.
13: epistēmōn en hymin — wise and understanding (sophos kai epistēmōn). The pair echoes the OT wisdom vocabulary: Deuteronomy 1:13 calls for 'wise, discerning, and experienced men' using the same two Greek words in the LXX (sophos kai epistēmōn). Anastrophē (way of life/conduct) is the word for the total pattern of one's life and social interaction — wisdom is visible in the consistency of the lived pattern, not merely in pronouncements. Prautēti sophias (meekness/gentleness of wisdom) — prautēs appears in 1:21 for the receptivity needed to receive the implanted word; here it is the disposition of genuine wisdom.
14: zēlon pikron kai eritheian — bitter jealousy (zēlon pikron) and selfish ambition (eritheia). Eritheia is a word with interesting etymology: originally it meant 'hired labor' and then came to mean the self-seeking behavior of someone working only for personal gain. In Paul it appears among the works of the flesh (Galatians 5:20) and the divided motives (Philippians 1:17). James pairs it with bitter zeal as the characteristic of the earthly wisdom described in v15. Pseudesththe kata tēs alētheias (lie against the truth) — not merely being untruthful but actively directing falsehood against the truth, treating the truth as an opponent.
15: epigios, psychikē, daimonōdēs — three descending terms for earthly wisdom's origin: epigeios (earthly — from the earth, not from above), psychikē (soulish/unspiritual — belonging to the psychē, the natural self, without the Spirit; Paul uses this word in 1 Corinthians 2:14 for the 'natural' person who cannot receive the Spirit's things), and daimonōdēs (demonic/of demon-character). The three-level descent is from natural to sub-natural to actively malevolent. The wisdom of jealousy and selfish ambition is not merely human but demonic in character — it participates in the disorder introduced into creation by hostile spiritual forces.
16: akatastasia kai pan phaulon pragma — disorder (akatastasia — the same root as akatastatos, the unstable double-minded person of 1:8 and the restless evil tongue of James 3:8) and every vile/worthless practice (pan phaulon pragma). The three instability-words (akatastatos/akatastasia) form a thread through the letter: individual instability (James 1:8), verbal/social disorder (James 3:8), and communal chaos (James 3:16) are all expressions of the same divided soul that has not submitted to God's wisdom.
17: hagnē, eirēnikē, epieikēs, eupeithēs, eleos, agathē, adiakritos, anypokritos — the eight qualities of wisdom from above form a compact ethical vocabulary. Hagnē (pure/chaste — ritual and moral cleanness); eirēnikē (peaceable — peace-producing); epieikēs (gentle/considerate/equitable — the quality of yielding one's strict legal rights for the sake of relationship); eupeithēs (open to reason/compliant/ready to yield — not the stubbornness of selfish ambition); eleos kai karpous agathous (mercy and good fruits — practical expressions of wisdom in community); adiakritos (impartial/without making invidious distinctions — the opposite of James 2:1-7); anypokritos (sincere/without hypocrisy — literally 'not acting'). The sequence from pure to sincere describes a wisdom that is both internally pure and externally consistent.
18: karpos de dikaiosynēs en eirēnē speiretai — the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace by those who make peace. The metaphor shifts from the immediate crops of earthly wisdom (disorder, chaos) to the harvest of heavenly wisdom (righteousness). Speiretai (is sown, present passive) — the righteousness-harvest is the result of what is sown in peace by peacemakers. The verse rounds off the wisdom-section by connecting peace (mentioned throughout the letter, but notably in James 3:17) with the righteousness that James has been concerned with since James 1:20. Wisdom produces its fruit in the environment of peace that only genuine wisdom creates.
